title: Getting It Together
speaker: Pastor David Chi
source_files: 8-4-24 -PM- Getting it Together - GUEST SPEAKER Pastor David Chi.txt

outline.title: The Letter of James: Overview
subpoints: Written to the 12 tribes scattered abroad — first-century churches in various trials
Problems in the churches: external persecution and internal sins (doubt, pride, discrimination, anger, etc.)
James's purpose: to encourage spiritual maturity and perseverance in trials
The letter begins and ends with patience (James 1:2-3; James 5:7)

outline.title: Context of James 1:22-25: Why Be Doers of the Word?
subpoints: The readers were spiritually immature — 'brethren' mentioned 19 times, showing James's pastoral care
James exhorts them to be doers of the word so that they will shine their light (Matthew 5:16)
Obedience in trials glorifies God and benefits neighbors — it is not for self but for God and others
The trouble we go through is the very context in which we must practice the word

outline.title: The Perfect Law of Liberty: James 1:25
subpoints: The doers look into the word carefully — 'looks into' is the same word used of Mary peering into the tomb (Luke 24)
The doers live in the word — they 'continue' or 'abide' in it
The doers live out the word — they are doers of the work, not just of the word
The doers choose to be doers — forgetting is not an intellectual problem but a heart problem
"Forgetful" in Greek means to ignore, pay no attention to, or overlook — it is a choice

outline.title: Two Liberties: The Encouragement of the Perfect Law
subpoints: The "perfect law of liberty" means the complete instruction of liberty
First liberty: the liberty of obedience — sinners are enslaved to disobedience; regeneration gives a new nature that desires to obey
Ezekiel 36:27: 'I will put my spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes'
Second liberty: the liberty of love — the walk of obedience is also a walk in love (Galatians 5:1, 16; Ephesians 5:1-2)
Obedience and love always go together: to obey God's commandment is to love God

outline.title: The Blessing of the Doer
subpoints: James says 'this one will be blessed' — future tense; God will bless in what we do
The blessing comes in the very place of the work: blessed in what he does, not merely in his condition
God is ready: he has saved us, given us his Spirit, given us the instruction of freedom, and given us the promise of blessing
The challenge: will you be a blessed doer of the word or a forgetful hearer?

summary: Preached by guest speaker Pastor David Chi during the Sunday evening service, this sermon takes James 1:25 — "But he who looks into the perfect law of liberty and continues in it, and is not a forgetful hearer but a doer of the work, this one will be blessed in what he does" — as its central text. The sermon begins with a broad overview of the letter of James: written to first-century scattered churches wrestling with external persecution and internal sins, the letter is a fatherly exhortation to grow up spiritually by persevering in trials. The main question is: why must we be doers of the word, especially when life is difficult? The answer drawn from Matthew 5 is that Christians are the salt and light of the world, and their good works in the middle of trials glorify God and benefit others. The sermon then carefully unpacks the four characteristics of the doer of the word from James 1:25: careful listening (looking into), living in the word (continuing/abiding), living out the word (doing the work), and choosing to do so (forgetting is a heart problem, not an intellectual one). The concept of "the perfect law of liberty" is explained as God's complete instruction for two intertwined freedoms: the liberty of obedience (sinners reborn to love God's law) and the liberty of love (obedience and love are inseparable). The sermon closes with God's promise of blessing to the one who obeys him in trials.
transcript:
## Introduction

It is my privilege and joy to bring God's word to you, congregation of Olympia, and fathers and brothers in our senate. Before we pray once again to ask God's blessing, let me just give you a roadmap of this afternoon's sermon. First of all, we want to think about the letter of James briefly. And then secondly, we are going to think about the section which you just heard from verse 22 to 25. And the main text that we are going to think about this afternoon is verse 25. So after the section of verse 22 to 25, we are going to zoom in to that one verse and think about that.

Let's pray. Oh, holy God, we thank you for this great opportunity to worship you and listen to your word. Your word is so powerful throughout generations. You save your people through this word. And through this word, you also have identified your beloved children. So Lord, that's what we want to taste, Lord, by your truth, by your word. Give us more grace. Build us up so that we would be wise and faithful servants for your kingdom. In Jesus name, we pray. Amen.

## The Letter of James

So as I prepared this sermon, one thing that I had not realized before was that this letter of James is not only practical, but also is very relevant to us. As James said in verse 1, this letter was sent to the 12 tribes that is the first century churches scattered abroad. And it seems that they were relatively well-structured, because chapter 3 talks about how they had some teachers and elders. So there was some structure of the church. But the thing is, those churches had many problems, many trials.

First of all, outside the church, there was heavy persecution. In chapters 2 and 5, James mentions the rich who oppressed Christians and even dragged them to the civil court. The world pushed those churches hard. And the churches suffered. But the struggle wasn't merely external, but also internal. Inside the churches there were diverse problems such as temptation, lust, double-mindedness, doubt, personal favoritism, discrimination, poor understanding of faith, hatred, sinful anger, tongue sins, pride, and these trials of selfishness, self-seeking, destructive criticism, judging one another, and worldliness. On top of that, there were some people who were pretty sick. And in the churches there were also some people who had departed from the truth of the gospel.

When we see this list, we could see why this letter is so relevant to us today. The problems that James dealt with in the first century are the very problems that we are struggling with in the church today.

Then the question is: why did James push all the possible buttons that he could push in this letter? I wonder if James pushes all the buttons because he wants to see his readers grow up spiritually by persevering, regardless of their life problems. James wrote this letter to encourage his readers to endure. And we can know that because the letter starts and ends with patience: chapter 1, verse 2 and 3 — "My brethren, count it all joy when you fall into various trials, knowing that the testing of your faith produces patience" — and the letter concludes in chapter 5, verse 7: "Therefore be patient, brethren, until the coming of the Lord."

And you know James's writing style — it is pretty direct. But in this short letter, James called his readers "my brethren" or "my beloved brethren" 19 times. This tells us how James cared about his readers. Yes, James's readers were a bunch of immature and simple people. They were supposed to be mature spiritually, but unfortunately they were not there. And as spiritual juveniles they kept causing trouble in the church. However, for James, those troublemakers were his dear brethren in Christ. As their pastor, James was concerned about their spiritual life. And so James directed them to deal with their life problems — real problems — in God's way, according to Scriptures. In doing so, they would be mature, complete, perfect, lacking nothing.

In spite of various trials and even of their failures called sin, James exhorted them to endure until they would receive the crown of life which the Lord has promised to those who love him (James 1:12). This letter is James's fatherly exhortation to the churches in various trials.

## The Context of James 1:22-25

So if we have thought about the letter as a whole, let's now think about the section where James 1:25 is written — verse 22 to 25. And verse 25 is simply the description of the doers of the word. The main point of this section is given in verse 22: "But be doers of the word and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves."

Before we think about the description of the doers of the word, I think it will be helpful for us to consider the intention of James's exhortation of being doers of the word. Why did James urge that his readers must be doers of the word, not hearers only?

The first answer is that the readers were not mature enough. They were supposed to do what God commands them to do if they were mature, but they didn't. So James basically told them, grow up.

But there is one more thing to consider. What was the general context of this letter? Various trials, inside and outside the church. That means James knew that his sheep suffered a lot. Then couldn't he give them some comforting words? Did he have to give them more pressure by saying "be doers of the word" while they were in various trials?

I wonder if it's because those 12 tribes scattered in the world were the salt of the earth and the light of the world. Commentators recognize the close connection between the letter of James and Jesus' teaching, especially in the Gospel of Matthew. And in Matthew 5:11-12, Jesus said: "Blessed are you when they revile and persecute you and say all kinds of evil against you falsely for my sake. Rejoice and be exceedingly glad, for great is your reward in heaven." And then Jesus continued in Matthew 5:16: "Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works and glorify your Father in heaven."

As a wise and fatherly pastor, James strongly exhorted the scattered and persecuted believers to be doers of the word because unless they do the word, they will not shine their light. According to Jesus, how would the world see the light of Christians? By seeing their good works — by observing the practice of the word.

Let me make this significance of being doers of the word personal, congregation. I do not know what kind of trouble you are going through this afternoon. However, one thing we could learn in this section is that you and I must practice the word of God, regardless of the challenges that we are facing. In fact, the trouble that we are going through in our life cannot be an excuse for being mere hearers of the word who do nothing. Rather, the trouble itself is the very place and context where we must practice and exercise the word of God as doers of the word.

But by experience, all of us know that that's not easy, right? When various trials discourage us and take away our motivation and confidence to serve the Lord and His Church, it is really, really difficult to render good and godly things as the Bible commands us.

When our life gets tough and all kinds of troubles consume our mental, emotional, and physical energy — if we obey what God commands us to do, then first of all, our God is going to be glorified. And second of all, God is going to use our good works for the benefit of others. In this sense, our obedience, our good works, are not for us. They are for our God, for our neighbors, for God's kingdom.

And why do we have to be doers of the word, especially when we are in trials? Is it because we are going to lose our salvation if we do not render our obedience? No — rather it is the opposite. Although our life is challenging, we will be doers of the word because we are saved. Although our flesh tempts us to do nothing and not care about others, we will deny ourselves and ask God to encourage and help us to render one more obedience. And that's because we are Christians who follow after Jesus Christ, who is the doer of the word. From the beginning of his life to the end, he obeyed the Father — by being incarnated, and by dying on the cross. Our Christian life simply imitates his life, the life of the doer of the word in various trials.

## The Description of the Doer: James 1:25

Let me read our text once again: "But he who looks into the perfect law of liberty and continues in it, and is not a forgetful hearer but a doer of the work, this one will be blessed in what he does."

In this verse, James depicts several characteristics of the doers.

The first is that the doers of the word carefully listen to the word. The verse begins, "but he who looks into the perfect law of liberty." The Greek word "looks into" is pictorial — it is the same word used in Luke 24 when Mary bent over to look into Jesus's empty tomb. That is the way that the doers should read, listen, and study God's word. These people give their full attention to the word of God. They chew on it, they dig into it, until they get the meat.

Secondly, the doers not just look into the word, but also live in the word. James continued: "but he who looks into the perfect law of liberty and continues in it." We can translate "continues" as "abide." Unlike the mere hearer, after listening or reading the word, the doers do not depart from the word. They remain, they dwell and live in the word.

Thirdly, the doers of the word not only live in the word, but also live out the word. James added: "he is not a forgetful hearer but a doer of the work." Did you hear that? James didn't say "a doer of the word" — he said "a doer of the work." For them, the word of God is not merely an idea that has nothing to do with your life, nor is it an option that you can pick and choose. For them, the word of God is God's authoritative command that they must obey. God's word and doing God's word cannot be separated. They always go together. For them, God's word is their life.

And lastly, probably the most important thing for us: the doers of the word choose to be doers. When James said "the doer is not a forgetful hearer," in Greek the word "forgetful" could mean "ignore," "pay no attention to," or "overlook." That means the reason that a professed Christian fails to be a doer of the word is not because the person is stupid. That's not an intellectual failure to remember God's word. Rather, that Christian becomes a mere hearer of the word because he chose to ignore, chose to pay no attention to, or overlook the word. This forgetfulness that James is talking about is not an intellectual problem — it is a heart problem. Being doers of the word is really up to you. God's commandment toward us is clear. God's grace is always sufficient, and the Holy Spirit always dwells in us and walks with us. So God is ready to help us to do his word. But the question is: do we want to be doers of the word?

## Two Liberties: The Encouragement of the Perfect Law

As a good pastor, James did not forget to give us some encouragement to be doers of the word. Where can we find the encouragement? In the phrase "the perfect law of liberty." I suggest that phrase simply means the word of God — the Bible. For James, God's word was the complete instruction of liberty. The word "perfect" could mean wholesome or complete, and for the original readers who were Jewish people, "law" would mean instruction — which is what the Hebrew word Torah refers to.

What kind of liberty does God instruct us through this great book? I suggest two things.

Firstly, it is our liberty of obedience. Think about the first four commandments — their main point is obedience to God. But do unbelievers love to keep those commandments? No way. Why not? Because they hate it. Sinners desire to disobey more than they desire to bow down before the Creator. Sinners are enslaved to disobedience. Satan calls disobedience "a good thing," "true liberty." That is a lie.

The true liberty that the Bible teaches is to obey God, to obey God's word, to keep his commandments. And because unbelievers are slaves of sin, they absolutely need a new birth — regeneration. And after that new birth, the redeemed people have a new nature in Christ, and now they started to love God for the first time in their life. Now they will have a zeal and desire to worship God which they have never experienced before. For some reason it makes them so happy to come to the church. It is so sweet to listen and read God's word. For some reason it is so joyful to pray to the Lord. Keeping God's law, doing his word, is not burdensome.

And God promises this in Ezekiel 36:27: "I will put my spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes, and you will keep my judgments and do them." If you struggle to choose to do the word, here is a wonderful encouragement: God promised that he will cause you to walk in his law, and God gave you his Spirit in order to empower you so that you will do his word freely.

And here is the second encouragement: the freedom of obedience is also the freedom of love. When we come to the New Testament, we see verses like Galatians 5:1 — "Stand fast therefore in the liberty by which Christ has made us free" — and 5:16: "Walk in the Spirit and you shall not fulfill the lusts of the flesh." The first fruit of the Spirit is love. And Ephesians 5:1-2: "Therefore be imitators of God as dear children and walk in love, as Christ also has loved us and gave himself for us."

Obedience and love always go together. When Jesus summarized the whole Bible, he said: "Love God and love your neighbors as yourselves." Obeying God's law is loving God and loving our neighbors. So if we have liberty to obey God's word in Christ by the Spirit, then we also have liberty to love God in Christ by the Spirit. Our obedience to his commandment is the very expression of our love for him, and we as Christians possess this beautiful freedom.

## The Blessing of the Doer

In this letter, James fully understood the heavy weight of our creation life. And after everything he has said, James concluded: "this one will be blessed in what he does."

Notice two things. First, James didn't say that "this one is blessed" — he says "will be blessed." He's talking about the future. And notice the very place that this one is going to be blessed: "in what he does." By faith, if you and I obey God and love him in the middle of our problems and trials, then God is not going to despise our obedience. Rather, he is going to bless us in what we are doing.

Brothers and sisters, God is ready. God has saved us in Christ Jesus. God has given us his Spirit to cause us to walk in his law. God has given us the freedom of obedience and love. God has given us his complete instruction of freedom. And God has given us this encouraging promise of blessing. God is surely ready to bless you and me if we obey him in various trials.

So what is your decision? Are you going to be a blessed doer of the word, or are you going to be a mere hearer? Let us not miss God's blessing. Let us be doers of the word. In doing so, though the trial is tough, let us keep knowing who God is: he is good, he is gracious, he is wise, he is wonderful. It is impossible for our God to disappoint us.

Let us pray. Our blessed God, how kind, generous and gracious you are. You know our weak and feeble state, and you also know how easily we are discouraged and disheartened. And so you provide us so many good things in this word that we will be patient and able to endure. Lord, we pray that you help us to persevere. As David prayed in Psalm 141, do not incline our heart to an evil thing. Teach us, O Lord, how to clean our hands, purify our heart. Teach us how to humble ourselves in your sight. You promise that God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble. So give us that grace to be doers of the word. Teach us how to be blessed by you in what we do. In Jesus name we pray. Amen.
